elements.sent-2411-001: Matching patients effectively and efficiently for clinical trials is a significant challenge due to the complexity and variability of patient profiles and trial criteria.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper presents a novel framework, Multi-Agents for Knowledge Augmentation (MAKA), designed to enhance patient-trial matching by dynamically supplementing matching prompts with external, domain-specific knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-003: The MAKA architecture consists of five key components: a knowledge probing agent that detects gaps in domain knowledge, a navigation agent that manages interactions among multiple specialized knowledge augmentation agents, a knowledge augmentation agent that incorporates relevant information into patient-trial matching prompts, a supervision agent aligning the outputs from other agents with the instructions and a matching agent making the final selection decision.
elements.sent-2411-004: This approach enhances the accuracy and contextual richness of patient matching, addresses inherent knowledge gaps in both trail criteria and large language models (LLMs), and improves the alignment between patient characteristics and the criteria.
elements.sent-2411-005: Patient matching for clinical trials refers to the process of identifying and enrolling participants whose health profiles align with the specific eligibility criteria of a given clinical study.
elements.sent-2411-006: Efficient patient matching is crucial to accelerating the drug development process and reducing the overall cost of clinical trials.
elements.sent-2411-007: It provides an opportunity for participants to access experimental treatments that could potentially improve their health outcomes and, in some cases, transform their quality of life.
elements.sent-2411-008: Patient matching remains a challenging task despite its critical role in clinical trials.
elements.sent-2411-009: Traditional automated methods, which are mostly rule-based, depend heavily on hand-crafted rules and are often time-consuming~\citep{brogger2020online .}
elements.sent-2411-010: These approaches are also highly sensitive to variations in how patient and trial data are expressed in natural language across different sources.
elements.sent-2411-011: Simple machine learning models often struggle with the load of information and the low density of relevant sentences in lengthy electronic health record(EHR) data.
elements.sent-2411-012: They usually underperform when dealing with complex eligibility criteria or inconsistent data formats, resulting in inefficiencies and mismatches that can negatively impact trial outcomes~\citep{kadam2016challenges .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The emergence of of Large Language Models (LLMs) offers a promising opportunity to improve patient matching.
elements.sent-2411-014: LLMs, such as GPT-4~\cite{achiam2023gpt , usually possess a large text window and advanced natural language understanding capabilities~\citep{karanikolas2023large , which enable them process complex trail criteria and patient data effectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, their general knowledge may not always be sufficient for knowledge-intensive tasks like patient matching in clinical trials, where domain-specific expertise is required.
elements.sent-2411-016: The knowledge gap inherent in LLMs and misssing information in the pre-defined criteria can limit their performance, highlighting the need for LLM augmentation of specialized medical knowledge~\citep{shuster2021retrieval .}
elements.sent-2411-017: While research indicates that LLMs do encode medical knowledge in a parameterized form~\citep{singhal2023large , this information can be outdated or misaligned with the special needs of certain clinical trials.}
elements.sent-2411-018: To address these limitations, we present a new framework called: Multi-Agents for Knowledge Augmentation (MAKA).
elements.sent-2411-019: MAKA is a novel architecture designed to enhance the patient matching process by dynamically integrating domain-specific knowledge aligned with the unique context of clinical trials.
elements.sent-2411-020: It consists of five main components: the Knowledge Probing Agent, Navigation Agent, Knowledge Augmentation Agent, Supervision Agent and Matching Agent.
elements.sent-2411-021: By leveraging this multi-agent framework, MAKA effectively identifies and fills knowledge gaps, enhancing the LLM's ability to understand complex eligibility criteria and patient profiles.
elements.sent-2411-022: This leads to an average improvement of 3\% on accuracy and 6\% on F1-score across various patient selection criteria, with up to a 10\% boost in more knowledge-intensive cases.
elements.sent-2411-023: Overall, our framework shows significant potentials for achieving more accurate patient matching.
elements.sent-2411-024: Traditional patient matching systems rely on query-based structured databases, such as Criteria2Query~\cite{yuan2019criteria2query , or handcrafted rule-based approaches like RBC~\cite{oleynik2019evaluating .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, these methods struggle to handle unstructured free-text data and are highly sensitive to variations in formatting and verbal expressions.

elements.sent-2411-027: Thus, the effectiveness and scalability of traditional methods are significantly limited, making them unsuitable for future patient matching tasks.

elements.sent-2411-050: The proposed framework, illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:framework , comprises five interconnected components, each with a distinct role in ensuring robust automation.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Knowledge Probing Agent: This component identifies knowledge gaps, both in terms of missing information in criteria and deficiencies in the LLM's understanding of clinical trials and patient characteristics.

elements.sent-2411-056: Knowledge Augmentation Agent: This component enriches the criterion with relevant, domain-specific information from different source.
elements.sent-2411-057: For instance, the Retrieval Agent~\cite{lewis2020retrieval retrieves information from indexed medical databases, the Online Search Agent gathers supplementary knowledge from online sources, and the Self-Augment Agent leverages the LLM’s intrinsic knowledge to refine the original criterion.}

elements.sent-2411-061: Matching Agent: Finally, with either the augmented or unaugmented criterion, as determined by the Knowledge Probing Agent, the Matching Agent evaluates patient eligibility in a zero-shot manner.

elements.sent-2411-094: One significant limitation of this study lies in the dataset used for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-095: The n2c2 dataset, the most commonly used, publicly available dataset for the cohort selection task, is relatively small in size and contains criteria that are neither particularly complex nor challenging for LLM-based processing.
elements.sent-2411-096: As a result, while MAKA is designed as a general framework capable of handling both simple and complex criteria, the dataset's limitations restrict our ability to thoroughly evaluate its generalizability across a broader range of clinical trial requirements.
elements.sent-2411-097: An additional observation from our experiments is that the Knowledge Probing Agent predominantly identified gaps in the criteria themselves—primarily issues of clarity or insufficient detail—rather than uncovering knowledge gaps in LLM.
elements.sent-2411-098: Moreover, the Navigation Agent consistently determined that all criteria could be sufficiently augmented by the Self-Augmentation Agent, with no need to employ external retrieval or online search augmentation.
elements.sent-2411-099: These findings suggest that the dataset's simplicity may have limited the full utilization of MAKA's modular capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-100: In this paper, we introduced MAKA, a framework designed to automate decomposed patient-matching workflows by leveraging the advanced natural language understanding capabilities of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-101: The framework's modular design incorporates five key components—Knowledge Probing Agent, Navigation Agent, Knowledge Augmentation Agent, Supervision Agent, and Matching Agent—each contributing to systematic knowledge augmentation and robust decision-making in patient-trail matching settings.
elements.sent-2411-102: We demonstrated that MAKA significantly improves patient-matching performance at both the criterion and trial levels.
elements.sent-2411-103: Compared to baseline approaches like Zero-shot and Chain of Thought (CoT), MAKA achieves notable gains in Accuracy, Precision, and F1-score, particularly when applied to complex or ambiguous criteria.
elements.sent-2411-104: By enriching clinical criteria with domain-specific insights, MAKA consistently outperforms alternatives while maintaining a balance between precision and recall.
elements.sent-2411-105: Further investigation of agent behaviors indicates that the performance improvements primarily stem from filling the knowledge gaps existing in the criteria, rather than in LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-106: Our results highlight the practical benefits of augmenting LLM capabilities with a structured muti-agent workflow for knowledge enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-107: For example, MAKA-augmented criteria improves understanding of clinical trial requirements by aligning better with diverse patient note styles and heterogeneity of criterion.
elements.sent-2411-108: The results validate MAKA's potential for real-world deployment in clinical trial workflows.
elements.sent-2411-109: It is worth noting that the design of MAKA supports the future development of a human-AI collaboration system.
elements.sent-2411-110: For the trial-centric patient matching task, the volume of criterion-supervision performed by the supervisory agent is relatively small and can be efficiently handled by human experts with minimal time investment.
elements.sent-2411-111: Exploring how humans can further enhance and facilitate this system presents an interesting direction for future research.
elements.sent-2411-112: To address the limitations of this study and validate MAKA’s generalizability, future work could also focus on evaluating the framework using more challenging datasets, such as those involving oncology trial matching which contains highly complex criteria or those with a cohort of larger size.
elements.sent-2411-113: Testing MAKA in thess contexts would not only provide a more rigorous assessment of its capabilities but also demonstrate its potential to address critical real-world challenges in clinical trial recruitment.

elements.figure-2411-007: [H] 0.5\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{|l|p{12cm}|} Criteria Definition ABDOMINAL Criteria: History of intra-abdominal surgery, including small/large intestine resection or small bowel obstruction. Explanation: Intra-abdominal surgery involves operations on organs within the abdominal cavity (e.g., stomach, intestines). Small bowel obstruction refers to blockages in the small intestine that may require surgery. ADVANCED-CAD Criteria: Advanced cardiovascular disease (CAD) defined by having two or more of the following: (a) Taking 2 or more medications for CAD, (b) History of myocardial infarction (MI), (c) Current angina, or (d) Ischemia (past or present). Explanation: CAD affects heart function due to blocked arteries. Definitions include: MI (heart attack), angina (chest pain), ischemia (reduced blood flow), and specific medications like statins or beta-blockers. A diagnosis of CAD alone does not qualify as advanced. ALCOHOL-ABUSE Criteria: Current alcohol use exceeding weekly recommended limits. Explanation: Weekly limits: up to 14 standard drinks. A standard drink is ~14g of pure alcohol (12oz beer, 5oz wine, or 1.5oz spirits). ASP-FOR-MI Criteria: Use of aspirin to prevent myocardial infarction (MI). Explanation: Aspirin reduces MI risk by preventing clots but requires medical advice to balance benefits (risk reduction) and potential side effects (e.g., bleeding). CREATININE Criteria: Serum creatinine level above the upper normal limit. Explanation: Creatinine reflects kidney function. Normal levels vary by age and gender (e.g., ~0.6-1.2 mg/dL for adult males). Elevated levels suggest potential kidney impairment. DIETSUPP-2MOS Criteria: Consumption of a dietary supplement (excluding Vitamin D) in the past 2 months. Explanation: Supplements include multivitamins, calcium carbonate, fish oil, or herbal supplements like ginseng. Excludes Vitamin D explicitly. DRUG-ABUSE Criteria: Current or past drug abuse history. Explanation: Drug abuse includes misuse of psychoactive substances, with potential consequences like health issues or legal problems. ENGLISH Criteria: Patient must speak English. Explanation: English proficiency ensures effective participation in English-based trials. Assume English proficiency unless noted otherwise. HBA1C Criteria: Hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) between 6.5\% and 9.5\%. Explanation: HbA1c measures average blood sugar over 2–3 months. Explanation: Ketoacidosis is a serious condition with high blood ketones, often related to uncontrolled diabetes. Symptoms include nausea, confusion, and rapid breathing. MAJOR-DIABETES Criteria: Major diabetes-related complications like amputation, kidney damage, or neuropathy. Explanation: Includes significant health effects (e.g., nephropathy, retinopathy) stemming from uncontrolled diabetes. Multiple minor complications combined may also qualify. MAKES-DECISIONS Criteria: Patient must make their own medical decisions. Explanation: This assesses cognitive ability to understand medical options, communicate choices, and appreciate consequences. MI-6MOS Criteria: Myocardial infarction (MI) within the past 6 months. Explanation: MI refers to heart muscle damage from blocked blood flow. elements.sent-2411-116: This paper introduces Multi-Agents for Knowledge Augmentation and Reasoning (MAKAR), a novel framework that enhances patient-trial matching by integrating domain-specific knowledge with structured reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-117: MAKAR consists of two key modules: the Augmentation Module and the Reasoning Module.
elements.sent-2411-118: The Augmentation Module enriches trial criteria by incorporating detailed explanations of relevant concepts to ensure clarity and completeness.
elements.sent-2411-119: The Reasoning Module then evaluates each health condition, following a structured, step-wise approach to determine eligibility and make the final matching decision.
elements.sent-2411-120: This paper enhances patient-trial matching by leveraging the agency and reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) through automated agent interactions, including collaboration, critique, and navigation.
elements.sent-2411-121: Experimental results on a public dataset demonstrate that our framework surpasses existing benchmarks, achieving up to an 8\% improvement in accuracy for specific criteria.
elements.sent-2411-122: Furthermore, in a real-world offline test, MAKAR achieved a 100\% accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-123: These findings show MAKAR’s potential as a scalable and robust solution for clinical trial patient matching.
elements.sent-2411-124: Patient matching for clinical trials refers to the process of identifying and enrolling participants whose health profiles meet the specific eligibility criteria of a given clinical study.

elements.sent-2411-128: To address these limitations, we present a new framework called: Multi-Agents for Knowledge Augmentation and Reasoning (MAKAR).
elements.sent-2411-129: MAKAR is a novel framework designed to enhance the patient matching process.
elements.sent-2411-130: The framework consists of two core modules: the Augmentation Module and the Reasoning Module.
elements.sent-2411-131: The Augmentation Module enhances trial criteria by providing detailed explanations and includes three key components: a Router Agent, multiple Augmentation Agents, and a Critic Agent.
elements.sent-2411-132: The Reasoning Module systematically evaluates each criterion-related condition in a step-by-step manner to determine eligibility and make the final matching decision.
elements.sent-2411-133: It comprises a Reasoning Agent and a Supervision Matching Agent.
elements.sent-2411-134: By leveraging this multi-agent framework, MAKAR improves the performance on patient-trial matching by dynamically incorporating domain-specific knowledge and step-wise reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-135: Our experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of MAKAR.
elements.sent-2411-136: On a public dataset, our framework achieves an average improvement of 3\% over benchmark methods, with up to an 8\% improvement for complex criteria.
elements.sent-2411-137: Furthermore, in an offline real-world data test, MAKAR outperformed the state-of-the-art closed-source reasoning LLMs and achieved 100\% accuracy, showing its reliability in practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-138: Our key contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-139: We propose an automated method to refine and enhance trial criteria, making them more interpretable and suitable for AI-driven patient-trial matching.
elements.sent-2411-140: We introduce a multi-agent framework that combines augmentation and reasoning, achieving strong performance across both public and real-world datasets.
elements.sent-2411-141: We identify a gap in existing approaches and highlight the importance of explicitly incorporating well-structured, detailed explanations of related concepts to improve LLM-driven clinical reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-142: In the healthcare domain, the application of LLM-MA systems is rapidly gaining traction.
elements.sent-2411-143: Yue et al.\cite{yue2024ct introduce ClinicalAgent, a multi-agent architecture powered by LLM reasoning for multi-agent collaboration, achieving higher precision in predicting clinical trial outcomes.}
elements.sent-2411-144: Similarly, Liao et al.\cite{liao2024reflectool propose REFLECTool, a multi-agent framework that enables agents to learn how to select domain-specific tools, thereby extend their capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-145: TriageAgent\cite{lu2024triageagent leverages LLMs for role-playing interactions, integrating self-confidence assessments and early-stopping mechanisms in multi-round discussions to improve document reasoning and classification accuracy for triage tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-146: Tang et al.\cite{tang2023medagents use multiple LLM-based agents as domain experts to collaboratively analyze medical reports.}
elements.sent-2411-147: And Li et al.~\cite{li2024mmedagent further introduce the multi-agent collaboration framework to handle multimodal tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-148: To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first to apply an LLM-MA framework for cohort selection in clinical trials.
elements.sent-2411-149: Our approach explore the agency and reasoning capabilities of LLM-MA to dynamically enhance patient-trial matching with tailored knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-150: Our approach allows a better contextual understanding of patient profiles and lead to improved matching performance and more informed decision-making in clinical trial recruitment.
elements.sent-2411-151: The proposed framework, shown in Figure~\ref{fig:framework , consists of two core modules: the Augmentation Module and the Reasoning Module.}
elements.sent-2411-152: The augmentation module is designed to generate criteria with comprehensive explanations of all relevant concepts, while the reasoning module evaluates each condition within the criteria to determine eligibility and make the final matching decision.
elements.sent-2411-153: Additionally, the framework contains five interconnected components, each outlined below.
elements.sent-2411-154: Router Agent In the MAKAR framework, multiple specialized augmentation agents are available to augment the criterion with domain-specific knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-155: The Router Agent determines the most suitable augmentation agent for the task and routes the workflow accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-156: Inspired by SELFREF~\cite{kadavath2022language and MOREINFO~\cite{feng2023knowledge , we adopt a prompt-based self-probing strategy.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: If the agent determines that the original criterion is detailed and understandable enough for other agents, the criterion will be directly forwarded to the Reasoning Module.
elements.sent-2411-158: Otherwise, the criterion is forwarded to the Knowledge Augmentation Agent chosen by the Router Agent for further processing.
elements.sent-2411-159: Knowledge Augmentation Agent: This component augments the criterion with relevant, domain-specific information from different source.
elements.sent-2411-160: Critic Agent: Acting as a supervisor, this agent ensures that the augmented criterion is aligned with the original instructions.
elements.sent-2411-161: For instance, the original criterion must be preserved verbatim.
elements.sent-2411-162: Additionally, the critic agent verifies that the augmented output is well-structured (e.g., concepts are clearly itemized) to facilitate further step-wise reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-163: This critic mechanism are designed to improve the framework's ability to follow instructions accurately while maintaining consistency and clarity.
elements.sent-2411-164: Reasoning Agent With the augmented criterion and patient EHR data, the Reasoning Agent evaluates patient eligibility by assessing for each concepts mentioned in the augmented criterion.
elements.sent-2411-165: It generates a detailed reasoning summary, including assessments for all relevant health conditions, and forwards it to the Supervision Matching Agent.
elements.sent-2411-166: Matching Agent: The Matching Agent determines patient eligibility by reviewing the reasoning assessment alongside the patient’s EHR data, making a final judgment in a zero-shot manner.
elements.sent-2411-167: Due to the sensitive nature of patient data, which is strictly protected under The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA), and the compliance requirements for processing detail clinical trial documents, full-scale online deployment is not currently feasible—particularly due to concerns about data security when using closed-source LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-168: Instead, to evaluate the effectiveness of our methods, we conducted two experiments: (1) an assessment using a public dataset and (2) a limited-scale offline evaluation with deidentified real-world data.
elements.sent-2411-169: The public dataset we use is from Track 1 of the 2018 n2c2 challenge~\cite{stubbs2019cohort , which is commonly used for evaluating patient matching in clinical trials.}
elements.sent-2411-170: The criteria in the n2c2 dataset are relatively straightforward and the patient records are well-structured, making them easy to follow.
elements.sent-2411-171: To evaluate the potential of our proposed method in a more complex scenario, we constructed a real-world dataset.
elements.sent-2411-172: Specifically, we sampled 30 eligibility criteria—15 inclusion and 15 exclusion—from ongoing breast cancer trials listed on ClinicalTrials.gov.
elements.sent-2411-173: We then manually selected 10 patients who have been diagnosed with breast cancer from our in-house EHR system.
elements.sent-2411-174: For each patient, we extracted their most recent deidentified clinical visit record.
elements.sent-2411-175: An expert subsequently asseessed whether each patient met each criterion, resulting in 300 labeled criterion-patient pairs.
elements.sent-2411-176: Notably, if a patient’s information was insufficient to determine eligibility for a given criterion, they were labeled as "NOT MET" to ensure a conservative patient selection.
elements.sent-2411-177: We listed the sampled criterion in Table ~\ref{table:criteria2.}
elements.sent-2411-178: We include two approaches in Experiment 1: the zero-shot method with hand-crafted criteria~\cite{wornow2024zero , as shown in Table~\ref{table:redefined criteria , and a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) approach~\cite{beattie2024utilizing using the original criteria.}}}
elements.sent-2411-179: In addtion, we also include o3-mini from OpenAI as the benchmark in Experiment 2 since reasoning LLMs have shown significant improvement on complex clinical scenarios\cite{xie2024preliminary .}
elements.sent-2411-180: Previous studies using n2c2 dataset have primarily focused on evaluating classification performance at the individual criterion level, often overlooking the overall patient-trial assessment.
elements.sent-2411-181: In this study, we also examine patient-trial selection performance in Experiment 1.
elements.sent-2411-182: To mitigate this, we synthesize a trial with selected criteria that had at least 100 patients meeting them, resulting in 28 eligible patients from a total cohort of 288.
elements.sent-2411-183: We then evaluated our method’s on this new synthetic trial in identifying eligible patients and reported the findings.
elements.sent-2411-184: Our experiments were conducted on an Amazon EC2 g5.16xlarge instance, equipped with a A10G GPU with 24 GB of memory.
elements.sent-2411-185: Unless otherwise specified, all agents in the framework are powered by GPT-4o.
elements.sent-2411-186: For external knowledge augmentation, the Online Search Agent uses the Perplexity AI search engine to fetch up-to-date information from the web.
elements.sent-2411-187: The Retrieval Agent uses all-MiniLM-L12-v2, a pre-trained checkpoint from Sentence-Transformer~\cite{reimers2019sentence , as its encoder to construct the vector database of MedDRA and FDALabel.}

elements.sent-2411-201: We compared its performance against zero-shot prompting with GPT-4o, zero-shot prompting with two reasoning models (OpenAI-o3-mini and OpenAI-o1) and CoT strategy with GPT-4o.
elements.sent-2411-202: Our method achieves perfect scores across all four metrics.
elements.sent-2411-203: Notably, for exclusion criteria, we report results for a corrected version of MAKAR (MAKAR$_{Corr}$).
elements.sent-2411-204: In the initial version, the model was not explicitly instructed on whether a given criterion was for inclusion or exclusion.
elements.sent-2411-205: Surprisingly, it was able to infer some exclusion criteria but focused on determining a patient's overall eligibility for a trial rather than assessing whether a specific criterion was met.
elements.sent-2411-206: For instance, if a patient was 75 years old, they should have been marked as "MET" for an exclusion criterion of "greater than 70 years old".
elements.sent-2411-207: While MAKAR correctly identified the age range and provided a logical reasoning process, it mistakenly output "Not MET"—interpreting the task as determining overall trial eligibility rather than verifying the criterion itself.
elements.sent-2411-208: The corrected version addresses this by explicitly instructing the model to evaluate each criterion independently rather than making a broader eligibility judgment.
elements.sent-2411-209: MAKAR outperforms all the benchmarks and the CoT follows closely, achieving 96.67\% accuracy and 92.94\% F1-score for inclusion criteria and 94.67\% accuracy with 93.65\% F1-score for exclusion criteria.
elements.sent-2411-210: Interestingly, Experiment 2 yields better overall performance than Experiment 1, despite its more knowledge-intensive criteria.
elements.sent-2411-211: We hypothesize that this is due to differences in the labeling process—the n2c2 challenge involves more subjective judgment, while our setup explicitly defines insufficient information as "NOT MET".
elements.sent-2411-212: Despite MAKAR achieving 100\% accuracy in the second experiment, we manually checked the reasoning results of all 300 patient-criterion pair and identified two reasoning errors that did not affect the final output.
elements.sent-2411-213: First, in Exclusion Criterion 13 (Patients with ongoing indications for cardioprotective medication—ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and/or beta-blockers), MAKAR reasoned: "The patient is on metoprolol, a beta-blocker, suggesting some ongoing indication for its use.
elements.sent-2411-214: However, without explicit information on the specific condition necessitating this medication (e.g., arrhythmia, prophylactic use post-cardiac event other than MI, or another reason), the justification remains uncertain." This reasoning was overly conservative, but the Supervision Matching Agent correctly classified it as MET, aligning with expert judgment.
elements.sent-2411-215: Second, in Inclusion Criterion 5 (Histologically confirmed breast cancer with an invasive component $\ge$ 20 mm and/or morphologically confirmed spread to regional lymph nodes(stage cT2-cT4 with any cN, or cN1-cN3 with any cT).), MAKAR reasoned: "The documentation mentions a suspicious breast nodule at the right breast (1.4 × 1.5 × 0.5 cm), which measures more than 20 mm in one dimension." This was an obvious error, as the maximum dimension stated is only 1.5 cm.
elements.sent-2411-216: The mistake may come from over-reasoning, possibly computing the largest possible diagonal as $\sqrt{1.4^2+1.5^2+0.5^2 =2.1 cm$.}
elements.sent-2411-217: However, the final decision remained correct since the criterion lymph node involvement still holds.
elements.sent-2411-218: In this section, we validate the contribution of each module in our framework.
elements.sent-2411-219: First, we remove the knowledge augmentation module to assess the reasoning module (MAKA-reason) using the original criteria, which essentially mirrors the CoT approach in Table~\ref{tab:exp2_results .}
elements.sent-2411-220: Next, we remove the reasoning module and evaluate the knowledge augmentation module (MAKA-aug) independently by performing zero-shot matching with the augmented criteria.
elements.sent-2411-221: The results, presented in Table~\ref{tab:ablation , indicate that removing the reasoning module has a greater negative impact on inclusion criteria matching (F1-score: 88.37\% vs. 92.94\%), while removing the augmentation module has a larger effect on exclusion criteria (F1-score: 93.65\% vs. 94.41\%).}
elements.sent-2411-222: Although we did not involve any reasoning-LLM-based agents, our approach still shows impressive reasoning capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-223: This can be attributed to the well-structured augmented criteria and the CoT strategy employed by the reasoning agent.
elements.sent-2411-224: For instance, when evaluating whether a patient meets the Inclusion Criterion 14 (Postmenopausal hormone receptor-positive patients), MAKA reasoned: "...the record does not explicitly state whether the breast cancer was hormone receptor positive.
elements.sent-2411-225: However, the use of tamoxifen, which is specifically prescribed for estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer by blocking estrogen receptors, strongly implies that her breast cancer was hormone receptor positive." Both MAKA and MAKA-reason successfully made the correct match, while MAKA-aug failed to capture this implicit relationship.
elements.sent-2411-226: Additionally, we also observed cases where knowledge augmentation improved performance.
elements.sent-2411-227: For example, in Exclusion Criterion 7, it mentions: "...all histological lesions were HER2 1+ or 2+, not detected by FISH amplification, and HR positive according to ASCO guidelines".
elements.sent-2411-228: MAKA-aug expanded the definitions from American Society of Clinical Oncology(ASCO) guidelines, made correct matches for all 10 patients.
elements.sent-2411-229: In contrast, MAKA-reason correctly identified only 8 out of 10.

elements.sent-2411-230: One interesting observation from our experiments is that the Router Agent consistently determined that all criteria could be augmented by the Self-Augmentation Agent, without requiring external retrieval or online search augmentation.
elements.sent-2411-231: This was true even for breast cancer-related criteria, which are typically complex and challenging for individuals without professional training.
elements.sent-2411-232: This relates to a limitation of our study, which comes from the dataset used for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-233: Both the n2c2 dataset and the real-world dataset are relatively small and contain criteria that are not particularly challenging for LLM-based processing, allowing all benchmark methods to achieve strong performance.
elements.sent-2411-234: While MAKAR is designed as a general framework capable of handling both simple and complex criteria, these dataset constraints limit our ability to explore the knowledge boundaries of LLMs and fully assess the framework's generalizability across a wider range of clinical trial requirements.
elements.sent-2411-235: Moreover, this observation raises a discussion about the specific improvements our framework brings to baseline LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-236: While the framework is designed to bridge the knowledge gap between trial criteria and LLMs' existing knowledge, our experiments indicate that LLMs can already expand and interpret the criteria in our datasets accurately.
elements.sent-2411-237: Instead, the primary benefit of knowledge augmentation lies in explicitly organizing relevant concepts and definitions in a well-structured format.
elements.sent-2411-238: By clearly outlining these key elements, the augmentation provides a type of breakdown of the subsequent reasoning steps, leading to improved overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-239: However, despite the dataset limitations, the effectiveness of the augmentation module remains significant.
elements.sent-2411-240: One of its key strengths is its potential to interface with diverse data sources, particularly those that are not publicly available, such as paid subscription-based medical databases or proprietary clinical trial repositories.
elements.sent-2411-241: Furthermore, it plays a crucial role in integrating the most up-to-date medical knowledge, including newly developed treatments and evolving FDA guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-242: This capability ensures that the framework remains adaptable and relevant, even when dealing with rapidly changing medical landscapes where LLMs alone may lack the latest information.
elements.sent-2411-243: By systematically incorporating external knowledge, the augmentation module enhances the contextual relevance of trial matching, making it particularly valuable in real-world applications where timely and comprehensive knowledge retrieval is essential.
elements.sent-2411-244: In this study, we introduced MAKAR, a scalable multi-agent framework designed to enhance patient-trial matching by combining knowledge augmentation with structured reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-245: By integrating an automated augmentation module with a reasoning module, MAKAR refines complex trial criteria and systematically evaluates patient eligibility.
elements.sent-2411-246: Our experimental results demonstrate that MAKAR outperforms existing benchmarks, including the state-of-the-art closed-source reasoning LLMs, and achieve 100\% accuracy in a real-world offline test.
elements.sent-2411-247: And it also achieves up to an 8\% improvement on complex criteria in a public dataset.
elements.sent-2411-248: Beyond performance gains, our findings emphasize the importance of explicitly structuring and expanding trial criteria to bridge the knowledge gap in AI-driven clinical reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-249: It is worth noting that the design of MAKAR supports the future development of a human-AI collaboration system.
elements.sent-2411-250: For the trial-centric patient matching task, the workload of criterion-supervision performed by the critic agent is one-time per trial and relatively small and can be efficiently handled by human experts with minimal time investment.
elements.sent-2411-251: To address the limitations of this study and validate MAKAR’s generalizability, future work could also focus on evaluating the framework using more challenging datasets, such as those involving newly launched oncology trial that required up-to-date medical knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-252: Testing MAKAR in thess contexts would not only provide a more rigorous assessment of its capabilities but also demonstrate its potential to address critical real-world challenges in clinical trial recruitment.

elements.sent-2411-280: This paper introduces Multi-Agent for Knowledge Augmentation and Reasoning (MAKAR), a novel multi-agent system that enhances patient-trial matching by integrating criterion augmentation with structured reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-281: MAKAR consistently improves performance by an average of 7\% across different datasets.
elements.sent-2411-282: Furthermore, it enables privacy-preserving deployment and maintains competitive performance when using smaller open-source models.
elements.sent-2411-283: Overall, MAKAR can contributes to more transparent, accurate, and privacy-conscious AI-driven patient matching.

elements.sent-2411-284: Patient matching for clinical trials is the process of identifying and enrolling participants whose health profiles meet the specific eligibility criteria of a given clinical study.
elements.sent-2411-285: However, three limitations constrain the performance of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-286: First, knowledge gaps~\citep{shuster2021retrieval may exist due to training data cut-off dates and incomplete coverage of specialized medical knowledge, such as newly approved drugs, treatments, or updated FDA guidelines.}
elements.sent-2411-287: Second, hallucinations~\citep{huang2025survey remain a significant challenge: LLMs are often prompted to generate detailed reasoning to improve interpretability and trustworthiness in patient matching~\citep{jin2024matching , yet such reasoning is unverified and potentially misleading.}}
elements.sent-2411-288: Finally, prompt sensitivity can substantially impact outputs, as even minor variations in prompts can lead to divergent results~\citep{zhuo-etal-2024-prosa .}
elements.sent-2411-289: To address these limitations, we present a training-free multi-agent workflow: Multi-Agent for Knowledge Augmentation and Reasoning (MAKAR).
elements.sent-2411-290: MAKAR consists of two multi-agent modules: the Augmentation Module and the Reasoning Module.
elements.sent-2411-291: The augmentation module enriches trial criteria by providing detailed explanations from different sources.
elements.sent-2411-292: And the reasoning module evaluates each criterion-related patient condition in a step-by-step manner to determine eligibility and make the final matching decision.
elements.sent-2411-293: We demonstrate the effectiveness of MAKAR our framework on public dataset and in-house real-world database.
elements.sent-2411-294: On average, MAKAR achieves an improvement of 7\% over benchmark methods, with up to a 10\% improvement on certain criteria.
elements.sent-2411-295: We demonstrate by experiment that LLMs are sensitive to how criteria are formulated.
elements.sent-2411-296: We introduce MAKAR, a double-module multi-agent workflow that allows complementary contributions from criterion augmentation and reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-297: And it achieves consistent performance improvement across different datasets.
elements.sent-2411-298: We demonstrate that MAKAR remains effective when deployed with open-source language models.
elements.sent-2411-299: And it has potential to contribute to more trustworthy and privacy-preserving AI application in drug development.
elements.sent-2411-300: To further motivate this work and demonstrate existing gaps, we conducted a preliminary experiment comparing patient matching performance on the 2018 n2c2 Challenge Track 1 dataset~\citep{stubbs2019cohort using three different criteria: (1) the original criteria provided by the challenge (see Appendix~\ref{sec:original_cri ), (2) human-refined criteria used by~\citet{wornow2024zero (Appendix~\ref{sec:human_cri ), and (3) GPT-4o-refined criteria (Appendix~\ref{sec:gpt_cri ).}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-304: Overall, the results in Figure~\ref{fig:motivation suggests three important observations.}
elements.sent-2411-305: First, the criterion sensitivity of LLM-based patient matching is substantial: simply varying the formulation of the criteria can cause notable differences in accuracy across most conditions.
elements.sent-2411-306: Second, while using human-refined criteria does not universally outperform the original criteria, automatically refined and augmented prompts generated by GPT-4o consistently improve performance.
elements.sent-2411-307: Third, explicitly incorporating detailed knowledge (e.g., explanations) into the prompt can further enhance accuracy, even when the LLMs themselves do not exhibit an obvious knowledge gap.
elements.sent-2411-308: For example, for the "CREATININE" criterion, GPT-4o was able to return the correct reference ranges by gender and age when being asked directly.
elements.sent-2411-309: However, explicitly adding these ranges in the prompt—as done in the GPT-4o-augmented criteria—increased performance by 8\% compared to prompts that used only general descriptions such as “normal range.” These findings highlight the need for methods that can systematically augment knowledge rather than relying solely on prompt engineering or implicit model knowledge.

elements.sent-2411-317: We use a widely used public dataset and an in-house real-world dataset to evaluate our proposed method.

elements.sent-2411-322: To assess our method in a more complex setting, we created a real-world dataset by sampling 30 eligibility criteria (15 inclusion, 15 exclusion) from ongoing breast cancer trials on ClinicalTrials.gov.
elements.sent-2411-323: We manually selected 10 breast cancer patients from our in-house EHR system and extracted their most recent deidentified clinical records.
elements.sent-2411-324: When information was insufficient to confirm eligibility, the criterion was conservatively labeled as "NOT MET." The sampled criteria are listed in Appendix~\ref{sec:clinicalgov_cri .}
elements.sent-2411-325: Previous research has shown that LLM-based approaches outperform traditional rule-based systems~\cite{jin2024matching .}
elements.sent-2411-326: Thus, this study focuses exclusively on LLM-based benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-327: We include three benchmarks: (1) TrialGPT~\cite{jin2024matching , (2) the two-stage retrieval-based zero-shot matching system from~\citet{wornow2024zero , and (3) the chain-of-thought (CoT) based method introduced by~\citet{beattie2024utilizing .}}}
elements.sent-2411-328: For the n2c2 dataset, we report accuracy and F1-score for each criterion as well as the overall average.

elements.sent-2411-331: Table~\ref{tab:main_result compares the performance of MAKAR with benchmark methods across two datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-332: Overall, the MAKAR model consistently achieves the highest or near-highest accuracy and F1-scores across most conditions.
elements.sent-2411-333: MAKAR improves accuracy by approximately 4\% and F1-score by around 7\% on average.
elements.sent-2411-334: Notably, it shows particularly strong gains on criteria such as CREATININE, HBA1C, and MI-6MOS, achieving improvements of about 10\% where other methods struggle.
elements.sent-2411-335: To better understand these gains, we examine the MAKAR-augmented criteria presented in Appendix~\ref{sec:gpt_cri .}
elements.sent-2411-336: Compared to the baseline human-refined criteria outlined in Appendix~\ref{sec:human_cri , MAKAR has two major improvements: (1) more detailed expansions of key concepts, for example, supplementing normal ranges for criteria like HBA1C and CREATININE across diverse demographic groups; and (2) more comprehensive and accessible definitions, such as using widely recognized terms like “heart attack” alongside the clinical term “myocardial infarction (MI)”.}
elements.sent-2411-337: These improvements improve the ability of LLMs to match patients from clinical notes written in various styles, resulting in substantial performance gains on these criteria.

elements.sent-2411-347: In this section, we validate the double-module design of our workflow on ClinicalTrial dataset.
elements.sent-2411-348: First, we remove the knowledge augmentation module to assess the reasoning module alone (MAKAR-Reason, equivalent to a CoT) using the original criteria described in Appendix~\ref{sec:original_cri .}
elements.sent-2411-349: Next, we remove the reasoning module and evaluate the knowledge augmentation module (MAKAR-Aug) using zero-shot matching with prompts detailed in Appendix~\ref{app:prompt_zero ).}
elements.sent-2411-350: The results, presented in Table~\ref{tab:ablation1 , suggest that both modules contributes to overall performance.}
elements.sent-2411-351: Specifically, removing the augmentation module (MAKAR-Reason alone) led to a 6\% drop in F1 score, while removing the reasoning module (MAKAR-Aug alone) led to a 5\% drop.
elements.sent-2411-352: To further demonstrate the effectiveness of the reasoning module, we compared its performance against other reasoning models.
elements.sent-2411-353: Interestingly, MAKAR-Reason outperformed both OpenAI o1 and o3 mini, which can be attributed to the chain-of-thought strategy employed by the reasoning agent.
elements.sent-2411-354: For example, when evaluating whether a patient met Inclusion Criterion 14 (Postmenopausal hormone receptor-positive patients), MAKAR reasoned: "...the record does not explicitly state whether the breast cancer was hormone receptor positive.
elements.sent-2411-355: However, the use of tamoxifen, which is specifically prescribed for estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer by blocking estrogen receptors, strongly implies that her breast cancer was hormone receptor positive." Both MAKAR and MAKAR-Reason correctly identified this case, while MAKAR-Aug failed to capture the implicit relationship.

elements.sent-2411-356: One key challenge for applying MAKAR in real-world settings is that agents in the reasoning module process patient EHR data.
elements.sent-2411-357: And such data is extremely sensitive and protected under The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA).
elements.sent-2411-358: It requires de-identifying large volumes of patient information before uploading data to a closed-source external model like GPT-4o.
elements.sent-2411-359: This process can be time-consuming and still have residual privacy risks.
elements.sent-2411-360: To address this limitation, we evaluated whether MAKAR can maintain high performance when replacing GPT-4o with open-source language models that can be deployed locally.
elements.sent-2411-361: Our local deployment experiment consisted of three configurations: (1)Zero-Shot Matching, where we applied open-source models directly in zero-shot mode using the criteria described in Appendix~\ref{sec:clinicalgov_cri and the prompts from Appendix~\ref{app:prompt_zero ; (2) GPT-Aug, where we used GPT-4 to augment the criteria (since criteria text itself is not sensitive) and then applied the same zero-shot prompt with a open-source model for matching; and (3) MAKAR, where we replaced all agents in the reasoning module with open-source models to avoid uploading data to externally hosted servers.}}
elements.sent-2411-362: The experiments were conducted on the ClinicalTrial dataset, and accuracies are reported in Table~\ref{tab:ablation2 .}
elements.sent-2411-363: We found that replacing GPT-4o in the reasoning module with locally deployed open-source models reduced performance by an average of 11\%.
elements.sent-2411-364: However, some configurations still have competitive performance; for example, MAKAR powered by the Qwen family achieved accuracy greater than 0.900.
elements.sent-2411-365: These results further confirm the effectiveness of the MAKAR design, which improved performance by approximately 5\% across open-source models.
elements.sent-2411-366: Another interesting observation is that smaller models appeared less sensitive to augmented criteria: models under 7B parameters showed no performance gains from augmentation.
elements.sent-2411-367: In contrast, both smaller and larger models consistently benefited from the reasoning module, with accuracy improving after reasoning was applied.
elements.sent-2411-368: However, this observation should be interpreted with caution, as it is based on a limited number of model configurations evaluated in this study.
elements.sent-2411-369: This work falls within the literature on patient matching using free-text patient records.
elements.sent-2411-370: Early approaches such as RBC~\cite{oleynik2019evaluating relied on rule-based classification.}
elements.sent-2411-371: Transformer encoder-based methods were later introduced, including DeepEnroll~\cite{zhang2020deepenroll and COMPOSE~\cite{gao2020compose , which improved the ability to encode complex eligibility criteria and patient notes.}}
elements.sent-2411-372: More recently, generative large language models (GLLMs) have been applied to this task and have shown stronger performance, as in TrialGPT~\cite{jin2024matching , CoT matching~\cite{beattie2024utilizing , and other zero-shot methods~\cite{wornow2024zero .}}}
elements.sent-2411-373: In parallel, \citet{loaiza2025transforming explored the use of multi-agent systems to analyze oncology knowledge graphs for patient enrollment.}
elements.sent-2411-374: In this study, we introduced MAKAR, a scalable multi-agent workflow designed to enhance patient-trial matching by combining criterion augmentation with structured reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-375: Our experimental results show that MAKAR achieves an average improvement of 7\% and up to 10\% on certain criteria.
elements.sent-2411-376: Through detailed ablation studies, we further demonstrate that both modules of the MAKAR design contribute independently to these gains.
elements.sent-2411-377: In addition, our local deployment experiments confirmed that MAKAR remains effective when using open-source language models.
elements.sent-2411-378: Even with smaller models, such as Qwen and Gemma, the MAKAR workflow achieved competitive performance.
elements.sent-2411-379: These findings suggest that MAKAR can play a significant role in enabling more accurate and privacy-preserving patient selection.
elements.sent-2411-380: One observation from our experiments is that the Router Agent consistently determined that all criteria could be augmented by the Self-Augmentation Agent, without requiring external retrieval or online search augmentation.
elements.sent-2411-381: While MAKAR is designed as a general framework capable of handling both simple and complex criteria, these dataset constraints limit our ability to explore the knowledge boundaries of LLMs and fully assess our method's generalizability across a wider range of clinical trial requirements.
elements.sent-2411-382: By systematically incorporating external knowledge, the augmentation module enhances the contextual relevance of trial matching, making it particularly valuable in real-world applications where timely and comprehensive knowledge retrieval is essential.. .

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{MAKAR: Multi-Agent Knowledge Augmentation and Reasoning} \label{app:alg} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Original criterion $C_{orig}$, Patient EHR data $P_{EHR}$ \Ensure Matching result $M \in \{\text{0}, \text{1}\}$ \State // Augmentation Module: MAKAR-Aug \State $C_{aug} \gets \text{RouterAgent}(C_{orig})$ \If{$\text{RouterAgent determines } C_{orig} \text{ is detailed enough}$} \State $C_{aug} \gets C_{orig}$ \Comment{Forward directly to Reasoning Module} \Else \State $\text{agent\_type} \gets \text{RouterAgent.selectAgent}(C_{orig})$ \If{$\text{agent\_type} = \text{retrieval}$} \State $C_{aug} \gets \text{RetrievalAgent}(C_{orig}, \text{databases})$ \ElsIf{$\text{agent\_type} = \text{online\_search}$} \State $C_{aug} \gets \text{OnlineSearchAgent}(C_{orig})$ \ElsIf{$\text{agent\_type} = \text{self\_augment}$} \State $C_{aug} \gets \text{SelfAugmentAgent}(C_{orig})$ \EndIf \State // Iterative refinement with Critic Agent \Repeat \State $\text{feedback} \gets \text{CriticAgent}(C_{aug}, C_{orig})$ \If{$\text{feedback.rejected}$} \State $C_{aug} \gets \text{Re-augment}(C_{orig}, \text{feedback})$ \EndIf \Until{$\text{CriticAgent accepts } C_{aug}$} \EndIf \State // Reasoning Module: MAKAR-Reason \State $\text{reasoning\_summary} \gets \text{ReasoningAgent}(C_{aug}, P_{EHR})$ \State // Generate detailed evaluation \For{each concept $c$ in $C_{aug}$} \State $\text{evaluation}[c] \gets \text{ReasoningAgent.evaluate}(c, P_{EHR})$ \EndFor \State // Final matching decision \State $M \gets \text{MatchingAgent}(C_{aug}, P_{EHR}, \text{reasoning\_summary})$ \State // Supervision validation \State $\text{validation} \gets \text{SupervisionMatchingAgent}(M, C_{aug}, P_{EHR})$ \If{$\text{validation.approved}$} \State \Return $M$ \Else \State \Return $\text{validation.corrected\_result}$ \EndIf \end{algorithmic}
